title: Signs Your Model is Overfitting (and What to Do About It)
concept: Overfitting has a recognizable diagnostic pattern — high training score, much lower validation or test score — and a set of general fixes: more data, a simpler model, or reining in complexity with regularization.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the real goal of training a machine learning model?

options.text: Generalizing well to new, unseen data
Getting the highest possible training score
Using as much data as physically possible

exercises.explain: Right! Now let's get practical: how do you actually spot when a model has drifted away from that goal into overfitting?

exercises.prompt: True or false: A validation set can help you notice overfitting before you ever touch the final test set.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Comparing training scores against validation scores is exactly how overfitting gets caught early.

cards.title: A doctor reading symptoms
cards.body: A doctor doesn't need to see a broken bone directly to diagnose it — a specific combination of symptoms (swelling, pain, limited motion) is enough to recognize the pattern. Overfitting has its own recognizable symptom pattern too.

cards.title: The tell-tale sign of overfitting
cards.body: The clearest diagnostic sign of overfitting is a big gap: a high score on training data paired with a much lower score on validation or test data. The bigger that gap, the more the model memorized instead of learned.

cards.title: Acing homework, failing pop quizzes
cards.body: A student who aces every homework assignment (which they've seen the answers to before) but bombs every unannounced pop quiz on the same material is showing the exact same pattern as an overfit model: great on the familiar, poor on the new.

cards.title: Diagnosing a shopping recommender
cards.body: A recommendation model scores 98% on training data but only 61% on test data — a huge gap, a clear overfitting diagnosis. The team's fixes: gather more varied training data, simplify the model, or apply regularization to discourage it from chasing every quirk (a technique this course explores in depth later).

cards.title: "Just train longer" isn't the fix
cards.body: It's tempting to think more training time cures every problem. For overfitting, it's often the opposite — training even longer can let the model memorize the training data's noise even more tightly, widening the gap instead of closing it.

cards.title: Diagnosis and general fixes
cards.body: Spot the pattern, then reach for a fix:

left.text:
Training score: high
Validation/test score: much lower

exercises.prompt: What's the clearest diagnostic sign that a model is overfitting?

options.text: A high training score paired with a much lower validation or test score
Low scores on both training and test data
Similar scores on both training and test data
The model trains very quickly

exercises.explain: Exactly! A wide gap between a strong training score and a weak validation/test score is the classic overfitting signature.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Training a model for even longer is a reliable general fix for overfitting.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Training longer often deepens overfitting rather than fixing it — it lets the model memorize noise even harder.

exercises.sentence: One general fix for overfitting is to make the model ___, so it has less room to memorize noise.
exercises.explain: A simpler model has less capacity to latch onto quirky noise, pushing it back toward the goldilocks zone.

exercises.prompt: Which of these situations should raise a red flag for overfitting?

options.text: A model scores 96% on training data and 54% on validation data
A model scores 82% on training data and 80% on validation data
A model scores 75% on training data and 74% on validation data

exercises.explain: Correct! That 42-point gap between training and validation is a textbook overfitting red flag; the other two show consistent, healthy scores.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a reasonable general fix for an overfitting model?

options.text: Gather more varied training data so the model sees a fuller picture of the real pattern
Make the model dramatically more complex so it can memorize even more detail
Remove the validation set so overfitting can't be detected anymore
Train exclusively on a smaller, narrower slice of the same data

exercises.explain: Exactly! More varied data helps a model learn the real pattern instead of memorizing quirks from a narrow slice.

exercises.prompt: A voice-assistant model scores 99% recognizing commands from its training recordings but only 58% on new voices during a live demo. The team has time for exactly one fix before the next demo. Which is the most sensible first move?

options.text: Simplify the model or gather more varied training voices — both directly address the overfitting gap they diagnosed
Train it even longer on the exact same recordings
Make the model even more complex to squeeze out a higher training score
Stop checking validation scores so the gap stops showing up

exercises.explain: Right! Simplifying the model or diversifying the training data both target the actual cause of the overfitting gap, unlike the other options which would make it worse or just hide it.

exercises.prompt: Two models are compared. Model A: 90% training, 88% validation. Model B: 99% training, 62% validation. A teammate says "Model B is clearly better since its training score is higher." How would you respond, using what you know about diagnosing overfitting?

options.text: Disagree — Model B's huge train-validation gap signals overfitting, while Model A's small, consistent gap suggests it will generalize better in the real world
Agree — the higher training score always wins, regardless of validation performance
Agree, but only because Model B took longer to train
Disagree, but only because Model A used fewer features, which is unrelated to the scores shown

exercises.explain: Exactly! A small, consistent gap (Model A) beats a huge gap (Model B) — Model B's flashy training score is hiding an overfitting problem that will hurt it on real, new data.
